---
title: "Neurosymbolic solver for deductive trading"
jupyter: brainvomit
---

## Thesis

A prediction market sells **contracts** that pay \$1 if a stated thing happens and
\$0 if it doesn't, so a contract's price acts like the crowd's estimate of the
probability. The familiar ways to make risk-free money here — prices that don't add
up, or the same bet priced differently on two sites — vanish fast once professional
traders arrive, leaving a race won by whoever is fastest.

This project tests a different kind of opportunity: one you find by **reasoning**,
not by being fast. Every contract has a **rulebook** — the precise legal text that
decides payout. The plan: use a language model to read those rulebooks and translate
them into formal logic, then hand that logic to an automated theorem-prover (a
**solver**) that can *prove* when two or more contracts' prices logically
contradict each other. A proven contradiction is a guaranteed-profit trade. The edge,
if it exists, comes from reading fine print nobody bothered to combine — not from
trading faster.

<!-- TODO: your framing / why this caught your interest. -->

Two things had to arrive together: **markets that trade yes/no claims** (logic needs
true/false statements to work on), and **cheap reading of dense legal text** (turning
exception-riddled fine print into clean structure used to take a human per contract;
language models made it cheap at scale).

**Induction** learns patterns from data ("it went up the last ten times") — what
essentially all quantitative trading does. **Deduction** proves something *must*
follow from rules ("if one party controls the House, the Speaker is necessarily from
that party"). Prediction markets are the first asset class where deduction bites,
because they trade true/false claims that can logically depend on one another.

There's also a reason we *must* stay with deduction: a market event happens once, and
for a one-time event a single "true probability" is ill-defined. What *is* well-defined
is **coherence** — whether a set of prices contradict each other right now. That can be
checked today, predicting nothing. Trust comes from a **division of labor**: the
language model does only the fuzzy part (reading and translating); everything with a
provably-correct answer goes to the solver.

<!-- TODO. -->

## Example scenario

Before any prediction markets, here's the same idea on something familiar. A World Cup
group is four teams in a round robin — a win is worth 3 points, a draw 1, a loss 0, and
the top two advance. Encode those rules once, drop in the table so far, and the solver
reads off things the table itself hides.

Going into the final round (illustrative): Brazil leads, and the two chasers — Serbia
and Switzerland, level on points — happen to **play each other**.

| Team | Points | Final match |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Brazil | 6 | vs Cameroon |
| Serbia | 3 | vs Switzerland |
| Switzerland | 3 | vs Serbia |
| Cameroon | 0 | vs Brazil |

The whole model is four lines of logic:

- **each remaining match** is one unknown — home win / draw / away win — paying
  `(3, 0)` / `(1, 1)` / `(0, 3)`;
- **final points** = points now + what those unknowns add;
- **clinched** ⟺ `UNSAT(2+ rivals can finish at or above it)` — no result leaves it
  outside the top two;
- **eliminated** ⟺ `UNSAT(≤1 rival finishes strictly above it)` — every result leaves
  it behind two or more.

You can also pin a single result and re-ask. Running it on the snapshot:

```{python}
#| echo: false
import z3

teams = ["Brazil", "Serbia", "Switzerland", "Cameroon"]
points = {"Brazil": 6, "Serbia": 3, "Switzerland": 3, "Cameroon": 0}
remaining = [("Serbia", "Switzerland"), ("Brazil", "Cameroon")]   # the two final-round matches

def model(force=None):
    s = z3.Solver()
    gained, res = {t: [] for t in teams}, {}
    for i, (home, away) in enumerate(remaining):
        r = z3.Int(f"match_{i}"); s.add(r >= 0, r <= 2)   # 2 home win · 1 draw · 0 away win
        res[(home, away)] = r
        gained[home].append(z3.If(r == 2, 3, z3.If(r == 1, 1, 0)))
        gained[away].append(z3.If(r == 0, 3, z3.If(r == 1, 1, 0)))
    final = {t: points[t] + z3.Sum(gained[t]) for t in teams}
    if force is not None:
        s.add(force(res))
    return s, final

def rivals_at_least(final, t):
    return z3.Sum([z3.If(final[u] >= final[t], 1, 0) for u in teams if u != t])
def rivals_above(final, t):
    return z3.Sum([z3.If(final[u] > final[t], 1, 0) for u in teams if u != t])

def clinched(t, force=None):
    s, final = model(force); s.add(rivals_at_least(final, t) >= 2)
    return s.check() == z3.unsat
def eliminated(t, force=None):
    s, final = model(force); s.add(rivals_above(final, t) <= 1)
    return s.check() == z3.unsat

for t in teams:
    print(f"{t:12} " + ("clinched" if clinched(t) else "eliminated" if eliminated(t) else "still alive"))

# pin a result: is Cameroon still eliminated even if it beats Brazil?
cameroon_wins = lambda res: res[("Brazil", "Cameroon")] == 0
print("\nCameroon eliminated even if it beats Brazil:", eliminated("Cameroon", force=cameroon_wins))
```

The two answers worth having aren't in the points column:

- **Brazil has already clinched — before its own match.** Serbia and Switzerland each
  sit on 3 and could climb to 6, level with Brazil — but they play *each other*, so at
  most one gets there and the other is left behind. No set of results puts two teams
  above Brazil, so it is through even if it loses to Cameroon.
- **Cameroon is eliminated even if it beats Brazil.** A win lifts it only to 4, and the
  Serbia–Switzerland game always leaves two teams ahead — so its fate is sealed by a
  match it isn't even in.

Both are *fixture* facts the standings can't show: the schedule couples the teams in
ways a glance misses, and the solver just reads them off. Forced, impossible, and the
conditional "even if it wins" query are the same moves the rest of this page makes —
swap "tournament rules" for "contract rulebooks" and "standings" for "live prices."

*(Points only; goal-difference tiebreakers ignored.)*

## Strategy

The expensive step — carefully reading a rulebook, turning it into logic, checking it
with the solver — is spent only on promising candidates, picked out by cheap filters
first. The pipeline:

> is it actually being traded? → translate rulebooks into logic → is the link
> *enforceable* (forced by the rules, not just true about the world)? → score how much
> the contracts pin each other down → grow the set toward whatever adds the most force

Two ideas do the heavy lifting. A **grounding** label attached to every claimed link
names *what* connects two contracts — which makes "merely true about the world" links
impossible to write down, and checks the resolver is actually obligated to honor the
link at payout. And the **force score** counts how many still-possible outcomes a
contract rules out, a set-level measure that can catch a link only visible when three
or more contracts combine.

Whether a search is even worth starting is decided up front by a break-even rule —
the fraction of groups that must contain a tradeable find to justify the cost:

$$p_1 = \frac{C_\text{cluster}}{V_\text{find}\times R}$$

where $C_\text{cluster}$ is the cost to examine one group, $V_\text{find}$ the profit
per find (capped by how much the market can absorb), and $R$ how many times one
discovery pays off.

<!-- TODO: anything you want to draw out about the architecture. -->

## The golden case

The relationship has to be **forced by the rulebooks** (not the casual descriptions),
**true no matter how the world turns out**, **hidden across contracts** (visible only
by reading two or more together), **honored at payout** (same resolver, same rules),
and **contradicted by the actual tradeable prices** — with real volume on every leg at
once. Conditions about history, outside copies, and the crowd all collapse into one
question: **does anyone already watch this relationship here?** If yes, it's priced.
So the target is one *nobody* watches — which makes any such opportunity temporary.

One rule, found by scoring category after category, decides the "true no matter what"
condition: **a forced link survives when both contracts resolve on a yes/no event and
breaks the moment either resolves on a continuous number.** A continuous strike (a vote
share, an inflation reading) makes the exact number the operative fact, so for some
values the implication between two contracts simply fails. A yes/no fact (a law passed,
a person appointed) is shared verbatim, so the implication holds automatically.

## From rulebook to proof

The fuzzy half (reading) and the provable half (checking) are kept strictly apart.
Here is the whole pipeline on real contracts.

**A clause becomes one formal statement.** A real Fed contract reads:

> "If the upper bound of the target federal funds rate … is greater than 5.25%
> following the Fed's Mar 2026 meeting, then the market resolves Yes."

The model's only job is to turn that sentence into a typed comparison, tagged with a
**grounding** naming what the resolver actually reads:

```json
{ "ticker": "KXFED-26MAR-T5.25",
  "var": "level", "op": ">", "value": 5.25, "at": "26MAR",
  "grounding": "adjudicator" }
```

A deterministic step then lowers that into the solver's language (SMT) — a real-valued
variable and one assertion:

```scheme
(declare-const level@26MAR Real)
(assert (> level@26MAR 5.25))      ; pays Yes  ⇔  this holds
```

**A relationship is proven exactly when its negation can't be satisfied.** The whole
engine rests on one check — a claim $p$ is *forced* by a set of contracts $S$ iff

$$\textsf{UNSAT}\big(\;\text{axioms}\;\wedge\;\textstyle\bigwedge S\;\wedge\;\neg p\;\big)$$

— no world consistent with the rulebooks makes the premises true and the claim false.

**Real verdicts**, each a single solver call on a known-answer pair:

| Premise(s) → claim | Result | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *Republicans win the House by ≥10 seats* → *Republicans win the House* | **UNSAT** | proven; prices must obey P(≥10) ≤ P(win) |
| *Mamdani wins NYC mayor* ∧ *Cuomo wins NYC mayor* | **UNSAT** | can't both hold (one winner) → P(A) + P(B) ≤ 1 |
| *Trump renames the Gulf* → *a Trump appointee is held in contempt* | **SAT** | **not** proven — solver returns a world where the first holds, the second fails; topical overlap only |

**UNSAT** is the prize: the statement is verifiably **true**, so any prices that break
it are a locked mispricing. **SAT** is the trap-detector — it returns an explicit
counterexample, exposing a "link" that was only topical (the independence trap that
kills most naive matches).

**UNKNOWN** is the third outcome, and it is informative. When a payout turns on *where a
continuous number lands* — a threshold like `level > 5.25` that must be reconciled with
another contract's `change == 0.25` — the implication can't be decided without extra
axioms tying levels to changes, and the solver returns UNKNOWN. A yes/no event never
does this. That gap is the [continuous-number trap](#the-golden-case) in its most
literal form: the logic that holds for discrete events dissolves the moment a free real
number sits between the contracts.

## Findings

### 1. Single contracts — dead end

In a 20-contract sample, about half had a gap between headline and fine print; **none
were tradeable** (each gap was harmless or already priced). Reading one contract is
easy, so the edge is gone.

<!-- TODO. -->

### 2. Economics & finance — almost certainly dead

Nearly every economics/finance contract also trades elsewhere (rate futures, yields,
official series), where professionals keep it priced — the **outside-copy killer**.
Sorted by whether an outside copy exists, the catalog splits ~3,674 dead (Sports 2,221,
Economics 572, Financials 568, Crypto 253, Commodities 60) against ~7,287 native to
Kalshi. The fertile ground is the native side — **politics and legal events
especially**, where one official ruling gets described across very different contracts
and the link hides in the fine print.

### 3. Contracts about the exact same number — barely exist

A 165-contract scan found essentially zero genuine matches on the *literal same
measurement* across families; most apparent matches just cite the same news source.
Expected — the real target is a definitional link between *different* measurements,
which by construction won't show up in a search for identical ones.

### 4. Scoring every category

A cheap surface-feature score (how native, yes/no, multi-contract, and recurring a
category is) ranks where to look: top — Entertainment, Politics, Mentions, Sci/Tech;
bottom — Elections and Climate (number-based) and Economics/Crypto (outside-copied),
self-consistent with the yes/no rule. The score only flags *where to look*, though: a
high value means many contracts share an event, **not** that their rulebooks force each
other — only reading them together (as above) confirms a real link.

### 5. Sports — good structure, killed by other exchanges

Sports has the best raw structure (many related yes/no bets on one game) and dodges
the continuous-number trap — but every bet also exists at a sportsbook, which keeps
the set priced. *Sports is economics with a yes/no coat of paint.* No category has
"native + hidden + recurring" all at once, so the target is a rare specific **pair** of
contracts, not a whole category — which is why a targeted hand-search beats betting on
a category.

<!-- TODO. -->

### 6. The narrowed hunt — AI-model rankings

A targeted search surfaced two AI-model-ranking series off the same leaderboard, one
framed **by company** and one **by model**, with the obvious link "a company's chance
is the sum of its models' chances." It failed three predicted ways: the prices already
agreed within the spread; the same crowd traded both framings; and the two lists didn't
line up cleanly. *Lesson: different framing only helps if it attracts two genuinely
different crowds.*

<!-- TODO. -->

### 7. The life and death of a real arbitrage

The strongest find pairs two contracts that look unrelated by their titles: a **parlay
on who controls each chamber** (the four House×Senate combinations) and a **direct bet
on which party controls the House**. Read together, the combos *partition* every
outcome, and "Democrats control the House" is exactly two of them — so the direct
contract must equal the DD-or-DR bundle. The solver proves that identity, then the real
Kalshi prices go in:

```{python}
#| echo: false
import z3
DD, DR, RR, RD = z3.Bools("DD DR RR RD")
s = z3.Solver()
s.add(z3.PbEq([(DD, 1), (DR, 1), (RR, 1), (RD, 1)], 1))    # exactly one House×Senate outcome
controlH_D = z3.Bool("controlH_D")
s.add(controlH_D == z3.Or(DD, DR))                          # the direct contract's rulebook
s.push(); s.add(z3.Not(controlH_D == z3.Or(DD, DR)))        # try to break the identity
forced = s.check() == z3.unsat
s.pop()
print("CONTROLH-D  ==  DD ∨ DR  :", "forced (UNSAT of its negation)" if forced else "not forced")

p_DD, p_DR, bid = 0.40, 0.36, 0.78                          # Kalshi asks / direct bid, 2026-06-21
gap = round((bid - (p_DD + p_DR)) * 100)
cost = (1 - bid) + p_DD + p_DR                              # buy NO(direct)+YES(DD)+YES(DR): pays $1 always
fee = sum(0.07 * p * (1 - p) for p in (1 - bid, p_DD, p_DR)) * 100
print(f"bundle DD+DR = {p_DD + p_DR:.2f}   vs   direct bid {bid:.2f}   ->   {gap}¢ incoherence")
print(f"locked basket costs {cost:.2f}  ->  +{round((1 - cost) * 100)}¢ gross,  −{fee:.1f}¢ fees,  net {round((1 - cost) * 100) - fee:+.1f}¢")
```

So the identity is **proven**; the legs are deeply liquid (real Kalshi open interest:
\$4.1M / \$990k / \$930k, ~1¢ spreads); two different crowds trade the two framings — and
there really was a 2¢ gap. It is the first concrete proof the target class is non-empty.
It just doesn't pay: the per-leg fee (~4.5¢ over three legs) is larger than the gap.
That's the general trap — a *liquid* mispricing is forced tiny, while a *large* one needs
a leg so mispriced it's either untradeable (thin) or merely slow (a speed edge, not a
logic edge). The profitable middle is squeezed shut.

**Was it real, and how long did it last?** Pulling the three legs' actual price history
from Kalshi, the gap (direct bid − bundle ask) sat open for about five days:

```{python}
#| echo: false
import plotly.graph_objects as go, plotly.io as pio
pio.templates.default = "plotly"
days = ["Jun 14", "Jun 15", "Jun 16", "Jun 17", "Jun 18", "Jun 19", "Jun 20", "Jun 21", "Jun 22", "Jun 23"]
gap_c = [0, -1, -1, 0, 2, 1, 1, 2, 2, 0]      # cents, from Kalshi daily candle closes
fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(x=days, y=gap_c,
                       marker_color=["#2ca02c" if g > 0 else "#cfcfcf" for g in gap_c]))
fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color="black")
fig.update_layout(height=300, margin=dict(l=0, r=0, t=10, b=0), showlegend=False,
                  yaxis_title="direct bid − bundle ask (¢)")
fig
```

Live from roughly **June 18–22, 2026**; then on the 23rd the D-House/R-Senate leg
repriced 36¢ → 38¢, the bundle rose to 78¢, and the gap vanished. A textbook *temporary*
edge — real while nobody was reconciling the two framings, gone once the price moved.
(Verified live: the [Balance-of-Power combo legs](https://kalshi.com/markets/kxbalancepowercombo)
and the "Democrats win the House" market are still open on Kalshi, now coherent.)

## Conclusion

Can't quit my day job — the tools work and Kalshi-native logical mispricings provably
exist, but none found so far is capturable: each dies to an outside copy, a continuous
strike, a shared crowd, an untraded leg, or a fee bigger than the gap.

<!-- TODO: your call on next steps. -->
